content.text: SLA Terms for Availability and API Latency

Service Level Agreement (SLA) terms for availability and latency govern what Anthropic commits to regarding the reliability and speed of the Claude API, and what remedies you receive when those commitments are not met. For enterprises where Claude powers customer-facing features, an SLA breach is a direct user experience degradation — understanding what the default terms actually guarantee, and what is achievable through negotiation, is essential to planning architecture with realistic reliability targets.
An SLA is not a reliability guarantee — it is a financial instrument. It compensates you for breaches but does not prevent them. Design your architecture to tolerate the downtime your SLA permits.
The Two Dimensions of Claude API SLAs

attrs.alt: SLA dimensions: availability uptime table vs latency metrics and targets

content.text: Claude API SLAs cover two distinct dimensions that require separate negotiation:
The percentage of time the API accepts requests and returns non-error responses. Measured monthly. Expressed as a percentage (99.9 %, 99.95 %, etc.).

How quickly the API responds. Measured as Time to First Token (TTFT) and end-to-end latency at various percentiles (P50, P95, P99). The most operationally important latency metric for streaming applications is TTFT P95.

These dimensions are independent and require separate SLA clauses. A vendor could meet their availability SLA (API is up 99.95 % of the time) while failing latency expectations (P95 TTFT is 8 seconds instead of 2 seconds) — both need to be contractually addressed.
The most important availability SLA negotiation is the definition of what counts as 'available.' Default definitions often exclude scenarios that cause real user impact:
Regular maintenance can consume hours of the SLA budget
Require ≥7 days advance notice; exclude only if <1 hour/month
Slow API may not count as downtime if error rate < threshold
Include latency SLA breach in availability calculation if TTFT P95 > 3× target
Only one AWS region is down; your API calls in that region fail
All regions you route traffic to count toward your availability measurement
'Acts of God' can excuse any outage
Narrow to specifically enumerated events; exclude vendor infrastructure failures
If your code causes 5xx errors, those are excluded
Reasonable — but the vendor must prove the cause within the incident report
Availability SLAs typically come in tiers, with service credits (partial refunds or account credits) triggered when availability falls below each tier:

Standard Availability SLA Credit Table (negotiate specifics):

Monthly Uptime %       Credit (% of monthly fees for affected service)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
≥ 99.9 %              No credit (within SLA)
99.5 % – 99.9 %       10 % service credit
99.0 % – 99.5 %       25 % service credit
95.0 % – 99.0 %       50 % service credit
< 95.0 %              100 % service credit (or right to terminate)

Negotiation targets:
- Move the 99.9% baseline to 99.95% for enterprise tier
- Add right to terminate without penalty at < 95% availability for 2 consecutive months
- Cap total credits claimable at 100% of monthly fees (not 50%)
- Require credits to be applied automatically, not require customer claim submission
- Set credit claim window at 60 days (not the typical 30 days — breaches take time to analyze)
How availability is measured matters as much as the SLA number. Negotiate explicit measurement methodology:
1-minute polling intervals are standard; 5-minute intervals can mask brief outages that still affect users.
'Available' typically means < 5 % error rate in a given minute. Negotiate this down to < 1 % for high-quality tiers.
If your use case uses only one model (e.g., claude-sonnet-5), confirm the SLA covers that specific model endpoint, not just the API broadly.
Some enterprise contracts allow the customer to use an independent monitoring service (Datadog, Pingdom) for measurement if it diverges from vendor reporting by > 0.1 %.
Why TTFT Is the Right Metric
For streaming Claude applications (real-time chat, copilot features),

is the latency metric that most directly correlates with user-perceived responsiveness. A user sees the first token appear in < 500ms and the response feels immediate — even if the full generation takes 15 seconds, the perceived experience is responsive.

End-to-end latency is heavily dependent on output token count — a 4,000-token response will always take longer than a 100-token response at the same output token rate. Negotiate E2E latency targets as token-count-normalized values rather than absolute numbers.

need contractual latency SLAs. Batch and async workloads are better managed by operational monitoring with internal SLOs (Service Level Objectives) rather than contract terms — negotiating latency SLAs for batch work adds complexity without proportionate protection.

Sample Latency SLA Clause (enterprise negotiation starting point):

Anthropic commits to the following latency targets for the
claude-sonnet-5 model via the Messages API (streaming enabled):

Time to First Token (TTFT):
  - P50: ≤ 400ms measured at Anthropic's API ingress
  - P95: ≤ 1,500ms measured at Anthropic's API ingress
  - Measurement period: rolling 7-day window

Latency credits are triggered when:
  - TTFT P95 exceeds 3,000ms for ≥ 1% of requests in any calendar month

Latency credit schedule:
  - TTFT P95 3,000ms – 5,000ms (≥1% of requests): 10% credit
  - TTFT P95 > 5,000ms (≥1% of requests): 25% credit

Exclusions:
  - Requests with system prompt + input > 32,000 tokens (latency not guaranteed)
  - Periods when customer's request rate exceeds provisioned capacity
  - Network latency between customer's infrastructure and Anthropic's API
    (measure from Anthropic's ingress, not customer's egress)
Never rely solely on vendor reporting for SLA compliance tracking. Instrument your own latency measurement from your application:
Availability and latency SLAs are the most visible, but
are often the actual binding constraint for enterprise workloads. Negotiate rate limit guarantees as part of your SLA package:
How many API calls per minute
Minimum guaranteed RPM at your tier; burst allowance
Input + output tokens processed per minute
TPM appropriate for your peak usage × 2× safety margin
Uncapped or very high daily limit for enterprise
count as errors in your availability measurement

if you negotiate them to do so — a critical distinction, since a vendor could technically be 'available' (not returning 5xx errors) while serving you 429s at peak load. Your SLA definition should explicitly include 429 errors in the availability calculation.
An SLA is only valuable if you can claim credits when it is breached. Most enterprises claim far fewer credits than they are entitled to because the claim process is undocumented:
Configure independent latency and availability monitoring (Datadog, Pingdom, or custom) that records every API call with timestamps, response codes, and TTFT.
At the end of each month, compare your availability measurement against the SLA threshold. If availability fell below the SLA target, calculate downtime in minutes.

Submit a credit claim within the claim window (typically 30–60 days from the incident — negotiate 60). Include: date/time of incident, your measurement data, the specific SLA clause breached, and the credit amount due.
Track credit issuance in your billing reconciliation. Credits should appear on the next invoice — if not, follow up with your account team.
Document chronic SLA misses as leverage in contract renewals: a vendor missing SLA three months out of twelve should face pricing pressure, not automatic renewal at the same terms.
Aligning SLA Tiers with Your Internal SLOs
Your contractual SLA with Anthropic is the
of your reliability posture, not the ceiling. You should operate with internal Service Level Objectives (SLOs) that are
so that you detect degradation and trigger remediation before an SLA breach occurs.
% of API calls returning 2xx per minute
0.5 % of requests = error budget for the week

framing converts the SLO into an operational resource: you have X % of requests available for errors before you breach your own SLO. When the error budget is depleted rapidly, alert and trigger your fallback chain. When it is being consumed slowly, schedule a reliability improvement sprint.
Beyond uptime and latency, enterprise contracts should include incident management SLA commitments — how quickly Anthropic detects, communicates, and resolves incidents that affect your service:
Significant degradation; error rate 5–50 % or P95 TTFT > 5s

Incident communication commitments are as important as incident resolution commitments. An unacknowledged outage leaves your engineering team debugging whether the problem is on their side or Anthropic's — wasting

. Require: a public status page with real-time updates, direct notification to a technical contact within the initial response SLA, and a post-incident review document within 5 business days of a P1 incident.
Price Stability as an SLA Dimension
Pricing volatility is an SLA dimension that is rarely framed as such — but for enterprises running large-scale Claude deployments,
unexpected pricing changes are operationally equivalent to latency spikes
in their financial impact. Include price stability terms in your contract:

Fix per-token pricing for the contract term (12 or 24 months). Protects against both increases and removes incentive to renegotiate when prices fall — take a 2-year lock only if current prices are materially below market.

Require 90-day written notice for any pricing change exceeding 10 %. Without this, Anthropic could announce a price change effective in 30 days — insufficient time to re-architect for cost efficiency.

If Anthropic offers the same or equivalent service at a lower price to another customer at comparable volume, you receive the same price. Difficult to enforce (pricing is rarely publicly visible) but establishes the contractual principle.
Pre-negotiate discount tiers at volume thresholds your roadmap will reach: current volume, 2× current, 5× current. Eliminates renegotiation friction when you scale.
SLA negotiation leverage peaks at two moments: initial contract signing and renewal. Between these, your leverage is low — Anthropic has little incentive to improve committed terms mid-contract.

Your leverage is highest. You can credibly threaten to go to a competitor. Use this to establish the SLA framework you want — it is far easier to negotiate 99.95 % uptime into an original contract than to amend it later.

Your leverage depends on what happened during the contract term. Document every SLA miss, every incident, every rate limit hit. A clean track record supports the vendor's renewal ask; a troubled track record supports your demand for better terms or a price reduction.
If your spend doubles during the contract term, you have leverage for a mid-term conversation about rate improvements. Proactively raise this with your account team rather than waiting for renewal.

Committing to 2× volume growth in exchange for better SLA terms is a credible offer — the vendor gets revenue predictability, you get improved service commitments. Only works if you have genuine volume confidence.
The best time to negotiate SLA terms is when you do not urgently need them — before you are in production, before an incident, before a renewal deadline. Urgency eliminates leverage.

SLA terms for Claude API availability and latency require separate negotiation for each dimension. For availability: negotiate the definition of 'available' to include latency degradation and rate-limit failures; push the SLA target to 99.95 % for enterprise tiers; require automatic credit issuance with a penalty-free termination right at sustained low availability. For latency: negotiate TTFT P95 targets by use case; exclude batch workloads from latency SLAs and manage them via internal SLOs; instrument your own latency measurement rather than relying on vendor reporting. Include rate limits explicitly in your SLA — a 429 error is as disruptive as a 503. Track credits monthly and use documented SLA misses as renewal negotiation leverage.
